articles.author: Cara Anna
articles.description: The ceasefire in the Iran war faces its most critical moment yet as the United States begins efforts to open the Strait of Hormuz. On Monday, the U.S. launched "Project Freedom" to guide ships through the strait, calling it a humanitarian effort. Iran views this as a violation of the ceasefire. The U.S. says two U.S.-flagged ships have safely transited, but Iran warns that any foreign military force in the strait will be targeted. Already, the United Arab Emirates says it has come under attack for the first time since the early April ceasefire, and a British military monitor says two cargo vessels are ablaze.
articles.title: What to know as US effort to open the Strait of Hormuz shakes a ceasefire

articles.author: Elaine Kurtenbach
articles.description: Shares are mixed in Asia after U.S. stocks set more records, powered by strong profits for major companies. Oil prices held steady after U.S. President Donald Trump said the U.S. would help ships leave the Strait of Hormuz, starting on Monday. Iran has rejected the plan, but Trump also said talks with Iran could lead to positive outcomes. On Friday, the S&P 500 climbed 0.3% to another all-time high, closing out a fifth straight winning week. The Dow Jones Industrial Average dipped 0.3%, and the Nasdaq composite added 0.9% to its own record. Apple led the way after delivering better profit than expected.
articles.title: Asian markets are mixed and oil holds steady

articles.author: Aniruddha Ghosal, Jim Gomez
articles.description: For millions of foreign workers in the Mideast, the Iran war has made life even more precarious. At least 28 were killed in missile and drone attacks before a fragile ceasefire took effect in early April. Now they face an even sharper version of a long-standing dilemma: Keep working in the Gulf, where wages are much higher, hoping that the truce endures, or return to already poor countries where prices have soared because of the conflict.
articles.title: Iran war sharpens the dilemmas faced by foreign workers in the Mideast

articles.author: Nell Clark, Mallika Sen
articles.description: Met Gala guests are interpreting the dress code “Fashion is Art” at this year’s extravaganza. Stars expected include Beyoncé, Nicole Kidman, Venus Williams and Anna Wintour.
articles.title: Met Gala 2026 live updates: Stars arrive on the Met stairs in art-inspired looks

articles.author: Beatrice Dupuy
articles.description: Fashion biggest night is making a statement this year with its dress code, “Fashion is art.” Global Editorial Director of Vogue Anna Wintour and the Metropolitan Museum of Art's Costume Institute curator, Andrew Bolton, are putting their stamp of approval on the idea. Guests from Beyoncé to Nicole Kidman are sure to make an artistic spectacle with the directive. A-list stars and cultural tastemakers are set to take the Met steps Monday as part of the fundraising event for the museum's Costume Institute. For an up-close look at all the red carpet drama, Vogue will host its annual livestream of the night starting at 6 p.m.
articles.title: Nicole Kidman, Naomi Osaka, Anna Wintour arrive at the 2026 Met Gala

articles.author: Michael R. Sisak, Larry Neumeister
articles.description: Blake Lively and Justin Baldoni have agreed to end their legal battle over the acrimonious production of their 2024 film “It Ends With Us.” The two sides settled their legal dispute on Monday ahead of a planned trial over Lively’s claims that Baldoni conspired with publicists to preemptively destroy her reputation after she privately accused him of sexually harassing her on the movie set. Baldoni directed the film and starred in it with Lively. He denied harassing her or orchestrating a smear campaign. Baldoni said the complaints about his behavior were made up by Lively as part of an effort to seize creative control of the movie.
articles.title: Blake Lively and Justin Baldoni settle their 'It Ends With Us' legal battle

articles.author: Maria Sherman
articles.description: Dolly Parton has canceled her previously postponed Las Vegas residency due to health challenges. She didn’t share too many details about her health but clarified that she’s always “had problems with my kidney stones.” She also said that her immune system and digestive system “got all out of whack over the past couple three years and they’re working real hard on rebuilding and strengthening those.” She clarified that her doctors have assured her “that everything I have is treatable, so I’m going with that.” Parton performs on occasion but hasn’t toured since her “Pure & Simple Tour” ended in 2016.
articles.title: Dolly Parton health update: Las Vegas residency canceled

articles.author: Luena Rodriguez-Feo Vileira, Bernard McGhee
articles.description: After U.S. President Donald Trump’s latest effort to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the United Arab Emirates on Monday said Iran attacked it for the first time since the ceasefire began.
articles.title: Live updates: US pushes to reopen Strait of Hormuz as Iranian attacks on UAE strain ceasefire

articles.author: Sarah Raza
articles.description: Nine Native American tribes are suing the federal government in a bid to stop exploratory drilling for graphite near a sacred site in the Black Hills of South Dakota. A small group of opponents has been demonstrating at the drilling site and at the mining company’s headquarters. The tribes filed their federal lawsuit Thursday against the U.S. Forest Service and U.S. Department of Agriculture, alleging that they violated federal law by greenlighting the project near a site called Pe’Sla, a meadow in the central Black Hills where buffalo regularly graze and tribal ceremonies and events are held year-round. The agencies and the mining company behind the project had no comment on the litigation.
articles.title: Tribes sue federal government over Black Hills drilling near sacred site

articles.author: Kim Chandler, Travis Loller, David A. Lieb
articles.description: Lawmakers in several southern states will be meeting this week to consider redistricting plans in response to a U.S. Supreme Court ruling that weakened the Voting Rights Act. Republican Alabama Gov. Kay Ivey has called legislators into a special session starting Monday that could enable a new date for congressional primaries. Tennessee Republican Gov. Bill Lee has announced a special session starting Tuesday to redraw the state's U.S. House districts. And Louisiana lawmakers also are in session to consider how to redraw districts after the Supreme Court struck down a majority Black district in the state.
articles.title: Southern state Republicans considering new House districts
